nodes.title: Cognate bridges: -tion / -able / -ible / -aire
nodes.explanation: Before we build anything, here's your unfair advantage. Thousands of English words ending in -tion, -able, -ible, and -aire are already French. Say them the French way and they're yours: situation, possible, nécessaire. You start with two thousand words in the bank.
nodes.street_mapping: These words don't change in casual speech — only the rhythm changes: even syllables, stress on the last one. 'Situation' is see-tua-SYON, not sit-you-AY-shun.

nodes.title: c'est + adjective/noun
nodes.explanation: 'C'est' means 'it is' or 'that's' — one little word, and with your cognates you can already say hundreds of things. C'est possible. C'est la solution. Point at anything in the world and comment on it.
nodes.street_mapping: 'C'est' is identical in street French — it's already the reduced form (of 'cela est', which nobody says). In fast speech it glues onto the next word: c'est important → say-tan-por-TAN, one stream.

nodes.title: Negation: pas (spoken norm is ne-drop)
nodes.explanation: To say something is NOT, real spoken French just adds 'pas'. C'est pas possible. That's how everyone actually talks. The textbook sandwich — ne...pas, ce n'est pas — you only need to recognize it in writing.
nodes.street_mapping: Drop the 'ne' entirely — spoken French negates with 'pas' alone. 'Ce n'est pas' becomes 'c'est pas', and at full speed it can squeeze to 'c'pas'.

nodes.title: je veux + noun / + infinitive
nodes.explanation: 'Je veux' — I want. Follow it with a thing: je veux un café. Or follow it with a verb in its natural to-form: je veux parler — I want to speak. One pattern, infinite sentences.
nodes.street_mapping: 'Je veux' compresses to 'j'veux' — one syllable, ZHVEUH. With negation the 'ne' vanishes too: 'j'veux pas'.

nodes.title: Greeting chunks: ça va, coucou, ça marche, ça roule, à toute
nodes.explanation: Five social passwords. Don't analyze them, just own them: ça va — how's it going, and also the answer; coucou — hey; ça marche — works for me; ça roule — all good; à toute — see you in a bit.
nodes.street_mapping: These ARE street forms — chunks live natively in casual register. 'À tout à l'heure' clips to 'à toute'. Answer 'ça va ?' with 'ça va' — the question is its own reply.

nodes.title: vouloir, full present (je/tu/il/on/vous)
nodes.explanation: Now the whole want-machine. Je veux, tu veux, il veut — all sound identical: VEUH. On veut — we want. Only 'vous voulez' changes shape. One sound covers most of the verb.

canonical_examples.formal: Est-ce que tu veux un café ?

nodes.street_mapping: je/tu/il/elle/on forms all sound like VEUH — the spelling changes, the sound doesn't. Street questions skip inversion entirely: 'tu veux... ?' with rising tone.

nodes.title: on replaces nous in speech
nodes.explanation: Here's a secret that makes French easier: nobody says 'nous voulons' in conversation. Real French uses 'on' — grammatically 'one', practically 'we' — and it takes the simple il-form. On veut. One syllable, no conjugation pain.
nodes.street_mapping: 'Nous + -ons form' → 'on' + the short il-form, always. 'On' is THE spoken 'we'; 'nous voulons' sounds like giving a speech.

nodes.title: pouvoir + infinitive
nodes.explanation: 'Je peux' — I can. Same music as vouloir: je peux, tu peux, il peut, on peut — all PEUH. Add any infinitive: je peux payer, tu peux venir. Can-plus-verb unlocks requests, offers, and permission.

canonical_examples.formal: Je ne peux pas travailler aujourd'hui.

nodes.street_mapping: 'Je peux' → 'j'peux' (ZHPEUH); with ne-drop: 'j'peux pas'. A question is just the statement plus rising tone: 'tu peux venir ?'

nodes.title: devoir + infinitive
nodes.explanation: 'Je dois' — I must, I have to. Je dois partir — I have to leave: the politest exit in France. Tu dois, il doit, on doit — DWAH everywhere. Obligation in two words.

canonical_examples.formal: Nous ne devons pas manger ici.

nodes.street_mapping: 'Je dois' → 'j'dois'. Street French often reaches for 'il faut' instead, but 'dois/doit' stays common and is always understood.

nodes.title: aller + à/en/au + places
nodes.explanation: 'Je vais' — I go, I'm going. Where? À Paris. Au restaurant — 'au' for le-places. En France — 'en' for feminine countries. Je vais, tu vas, il va, on va — movement sorted.

canonical_examples.english: Are you going to the restaurant?
canonical_examples.formal: Est-ce que tu vas au restaurant ?

canonical_examples.english: Are you (plural) going to the cinema?
I'm not going to the office.
canonical_examples.formal: Je ne vais pas au bureau.

nodes.street_mapping: 'Je vais' → 'j'vais'. Places clip in casual speech: restaurant → resto, cinéma → ciné, après-midi → aprem.

nodes.title: Futur proche: aller + infinitive
nodes.explanation: Here's the entire future, for free: aller plus a verb. Je vais manger — I'm going to eat. You already conjugate aller, so you can now say anything in the future. On va voir — we'll see.
nodes.street_mapping: Same reductions as aller: 'j'vais manger', and with ne-drop 'j'vais pas payer'. This IS the spoken future — the written future tense can wait.

nodes.title: Questions: intonation-first, est-ce que second, inversion recognition-only
nodes.explanation: Spoken French questions are just statements with a rising tone. Tu veux un café ? Question words go at the end: tu vas où ? Need formal? Glue 'est-ce que' on the front. Inversion — voulez-vous — recognize it, never build it.

canonical_examples.formal: Est-ce que tu veux un café ?

nodes.street_mapping: Statement + rising pitch = question. Question words sit at the end: quoi, où, quand, combien. 'Est-ce que' is the neutral fallback; inversion is for writing and formal speech only.

nodes.title: Filler chunks: bah, du coup, genre, en fait, bref, quoi
nodes.explanation: The sounds of a real French speaker thinking: bah — well; du coup — so, as a result; genre — like; en fait — actually; bref — anyway; quoi — tacked on the end for emphasis. Sprinkle, don't analyze.

canonical_examples.formal: En réalité, ce n'est pas possible.

nodes.street_mapping: Fillers ARE street register. 'Oui' relaxes to 'ouais'. End-of-sentence 'quoi' ≈ 'you know'. 'Du coup' is the duct tape of French conversation — it connects everything.

nodes.title: Direct object pronouns: le/la/les placement
nodes.explanation: Le, la, les — him, her, it, them — and they jump IN FRONT of the verb. Je le veux — I want it, literally 'I it want'. Get used to the little word arriving early; that's the French rhythm.

canonical_examples.formal: Je ne peux pas le payer.

nodes.street_mapping: The pronoun sits before the verb; in fast speech its vowel often drops: 'j'le veux' → ZHLUH-VEUH, 'on l'veut pas'.

nodes.title: Indirect object pronouns: me/te/lui/leur
nodes.explanation: Me, te, lui, leur — to me, to you, to him or her, to them. Same trick: they land before the verb. Tu me parles — you're talking to me. Je lui parle — I'm talking to him... or her — lui does both.

canonical_examples.english: We're going to talk to them.

nodes.street_mapping: 'me/te' squeeze to m'/t' — before vowels always, and before consonants in fast speech: 'j'te jure', 'tu m'dis'. 'Lui' covers him AND her.

nodes.title: y and en
nodes.explanation: Two tiny super-pronouns. Y means 'there': j'y vais — I'm off. En means 'of it, some': j'en veux — I want some. And the essential mood sentence: j'en peux plus — I can't take it anymore.
nodes.street_mapping: The 'il y a' family collapses: 'il y en a' → 'y'en a'. Formal 'je n'en peux plus' → street 'j'en peux plus' — one of the most-said sentences in French.

nodes.title: Passé composé with avoir, high-frequency participles
nodes.explanation: The past in French is a two-piece: avoir plus a past participle. J'ai mangé — I ate, I have eaten, same thing. Learn a handful of participles — vu, fait, dit, fini, compris — and your whole story goes into the past.
nodes.street_mapping: 'Tu as' → 't'as', always, in speech: 't'as vu ?', 't'as fini ?'. Negation drops 'ne': 'j'ai pas compris'.

nodes.title: Passé composé with être (movement verbs)
nodes.explanation: A small club of verbs — the coming-and-going verbs — take être in the past instead. Je suis allé — I went. Tu es parti — you left. Same two-piece idea, different helper, and the participle agrees like an adjective.
nodes.street_mapping: 'Je suis' → 'j'suis' (sounds like CHUI); 'tu es' → 't'es': 't'es parti ?'. 'On est' runs together with liaison: on-nay.

nodes.title: Connected speech: t'as, t'es, chais pas, y'a, liaison basics
nodes.explanation: Now the sound of real French. Tu as → t'as. Tu es → t'es. Il y a → y'a. Je ne sais pas → chais pas. These aren't slang, they're just how French is pronounced at speed. Your ear needs them even more than your mouth does.

canonical_examples.formal: Il n'y a pas de taxi.
Est-ce que tu as un euro ?

nodes.street_mapping: The core rules: tu + vowel → t' (t'as, t'es); il y a → y'a; je sais pas → chais pas; je suis → j'suis/chuis. Slow-formal and fast-street are the same sentence wearing different clothes.

nodes.title: Reaction chunks: grave, carrément, c'est chaud, n'importe quoi, ça me saoule
nodes.explanation: Your reaction kit: grave — totally; carrément — absolutely; c'est chaud — that's rough, that's risky; n'importe quoi — nonsense!; ça me saoule — it's driving me nuts. Instant authentic responses, zero grammar.
nodes.street_mapping: All native street register. 'Grave' as a one-word answer means 'totally'. 'Ça me saoule' → 'ça m'saoule' in fast speech.

nodes.title: faire expressions: weather, ça fait, faire + infinitive
nodes.explanation: Faire — to make or do — is France's Swiss-army verb. Weather: il fait beau. Time: ça fait deux ans — it's been two years. Even 'you make me laugh': tu me fais rire. When in doubt, French uses faire.
nodes.street_mapping: Street drops the dummy 'il' in weather: 'fait beau', 'fait chaud'. 'Tu fais quoi ?' is the default 'what are you up to?'

nodes.title: Reflexives: je me... with j'me reduction
nodes.explanation: Some verbs point back at you: je me lève — I get up; je m'appelle — I call myself. The 'me' is glued before the verb, and in real speech it shrinks: j'me lève. One extra syllable, a whole new verb family.

canonical_examples.formal: Je me lève à sept heures.

nodes.street_mapping: The reflexive bit compresses to a bare consonant: 'je me' → 'j'me', 'tu te' → 'tu t'', 'nous nous' → 'on s''. 'On s'voit' — see you around — is pure street gold.

nodes.title: Imparfait vs passé composé: storytelling contrast
nodes.explanation: Two pasts, one movie: the imparfait is the scenery — c'était, il faisait beau, j'étais fatigué. The passé composé is the action that happens: il est arrivé. Background rolls in imparfait; events land in passé composé.

canonical_examples.english: I was eating when you arrived.
canonical_examples.formal: Je mangeais quand tu es arrivé.

canonical_examples.english: We were at the restaurant when he arrived.
canonical_examples.formal: Nous étions au restaurant quand il est arrivé.
canonical_examples.street: On était au resto quand il est arrivé.

nodes.street_mapping: Same reductions as everywhere: 'c'était pas', 't'es arrivé', 'j'voulais'. Ear-cue: the -ais/-ait endings all sound like EH — that EH sound means background.

nodes.title: Relative clauses: qui and que
nodes.explanation: Glue two thoughts with qui or que. Qui when a verb comes next: la fille qui parle — the girl who's speaking. Que when a new subject follows: le café que je veux — the coffee that I want. Verb after? qui. Subject after? que.

canonical_examples.formal: C'est le film que j'ai vu.

nodes.street_mapping: 'que' squashes to qu' before anything: 'c'que j'veux'. Street also swaps the nouns: homme → mec, quelque chose → un truc.

nodes.title: Conditional softeners: je voudrais, ça serait, politeness register
nodes.explanation: French politeness is one tense away. Je veux — I want — becomes je voudrais — I'd like. Ça serait super — that would be great. Tu pourrais...? — could you...? The -rais sound is your soft-focus filter.
nodes.street_mapping: 'serait' → 's'rait', 'je voudrais' → 'j'voudrais'. Politeness survives street register — softeners are used at every level, just with the usual reductions.
